updated: Curated approved list; added Leadership in the Imagination Age and 2026-W25 items

items.title: The Futures Field: Functions, Forms, and Critical Issues
items.why: A classic reference for the basic orientation of futures studies around possible, probable, and preferable futures. It gives useful background for why futures research is not simple prediction.

items.title: Causal Layered Analysis: Poststructuralism as Method
items.why: One of the central methodological texts in futures studies. It is important for thinking about layers of futures: litany, systems, worldview, and myth/metaphor.

items.title: Foundations of Futures Studies
items.why: A broad foundation for futures studies as a field. It is useful when asking what kind of knowledge, judgement, and responsibility futures research can have.

items.title: An outline of future-oriented dialectics
items.why: A relevant conceptual paper for thinking about trajectories, tensions, and dialectical positions in futures research. It connects well with work that treats opposed futures as productive material.

items.title: Techniques of futuring: On how imagined futures become socially performative
items.authors: Jasper Oomen, Jesse Hoffman & Maarten A. Hajer
items.why: Useful for understanding futures not only as images, but as practices and techniques that can shape social reality.

items.title: Does the intuitive logics method – and its recent enhancements – produce effective scenarios?
items.authors: George Wright, Ron Bradfield & George Cairns
items.why: A useful quality discussion for scenario methods. It belongs to the methodological background for asking what makes scenario work actually helpful.

items.title: The critical role of history in scenario thinking
items.authors: Ron Bradfield, James Derbyshire & George Wright
items.why: A strong bridge between historical reasoning and scenario work. It is relevant for thinking about how the past can help causal analysis of the future without assuming repetition.

items.title: Unifying weak signals definitions to improve construct understanding
items.authors: B. L. van Veen & J. R. Ortt
items.why: Relevant for horizon scanning because it clarifies a frequently used but often unclear concept. A scan needs concepts that are not too loose.

items.title: Introduction to narrative for futures studies
items.why: A useful entry point for understanding what narrative can do in futures studies and why narrative is more than decoration for scenarios.

items.title: Narrative foresight
items.why: A central paper for narrative foresight. It helps explain why futures are not only analysed but also told, negotiated, and lived through stories.

items.title: Creating narrative scenarios: Science fiction prototyping at Emerge
items.why: Shows how narrative scenario work and science fiction prototyping can make futures more concrete and discussable.

items.title: Futures literacy through narrative
items.authors: Genevieve Liveley, William Slocombe & Emily Spiers
items.why: Connects narrative with futures literacy. It is helpful for thinking about how people learn to work with futures through story, temporality, and imagination.

items.title: Futures in Action: Expectations, Imaginaries and Narratives of the Future
items.why: Useful for understanding how futures, imaginaries, expectations, and narratives shape action. It gives a social-theoretical neighbour to narrative foresight.

items.title: Leadership in the Imagination Age: Navigating Unprecedented Possibilities and Moral Challenges
items.why: A useful bridge between leadership, imagination, storytelling, ethics, systems thinking, and futures studies. I placed it in Narrative foresight because its strongest radar value is how it treats leadership as orientation, moral imagination, and world-shaping storytelling under uncertainty.

items.title: The Delphi Method: Techniques and Applications
items.why: A foundational volume for Delphi as a method tradition. It gives historical and methodological background for more specific Delphi designs.

items.title: Disaggregative policy Delphi: Using cluster analysis as a tool for systematic scenario formation
items.why: A major example of developing Delphi beyond simple consensus. It is useful background for seeing how Delphi can handle plural viewpoints.

items.title: The Delphi technique: Past, present, and future prospects — Introduction to the special issue
items.why: A compact overview of Delphi as a continuing methodological tradition, useful for locating new Delphi variants inside the wider field.

items.title: Review of Delphi-based scenario studies: Quality and design considerations
items.authors: Markus Nowack, Jan Endrikat & Edeltraud Guenther
items.why: A useful methodological review for how Delphi and scenario work have been combined, and what quality problems need attention.

items.title: Improving the Delphi process: Lessons from social psychological research
items.why: Helpful for improving Delphi design through attention to judgement, group processes, and expert reasoning.

items.title: Consensus measurement in Delphi studies: Review and implications for future quality assurance
items.why: Important for the usual consensus-oriented side of Delphi. It is also useful when contrasting consensus with disagreement, tension, and dialectic designs.

items.topic: Philosophy of science and futures of science
items.title: The Structure of Scientific Revolutions
items.why: Still central for thinking about paradigms, scientific change, normal science, and rupture. It is unavoidable background for futures of science.

items.topic: Philosophy of science and futures of science
items.title: Representing and Intervening
items.why: Important for philosophy of science because it shifts attention to intervention, experiment, and scientific practice, not only representation.

items.topic: Philosophy of science and futures of science
items.title: Scientific Realism: How Science Tracks Truth
items.why: A central defence of scientific realism. It matters for futures of science because assumptions about continuity and truth shape how scientific futures are imagined.

items.title: Exceeding Our Grasp: Science, History, and the Problem of Unconceived Alternatives
items.why: Central for thinking about unconceived alternatives and the limits of our ability to imagine serious alternatives to present theories or worlds.

items.title: Darwin Deleted: Imagining a World without Darwin
items.why: A substantial example of counterfactual history of science. It helps show how alternative histories can illuminate what did and did not depend on a single figure.

items.title: Science As It Could Have Been
items.authors: Léna Soler, Emiliano Trizio & Andrew Pickering (eds.)
items.why: A major collection on contingency and inevitability in science. It gives background for asking how different science could have been.

items.title: So close no matter how far: counterfactuals in history of science and the inevitability/contingency controversy
items.why: Important for the problem that counterfactual histories may be pulled back toward the actual present. It is highly relevant for limits of conceivability.

items.title: Building futures scenarios for universities and higher education: An international approach
items.why: Useful for the methodological tradition of thinking about universities through scenarios and international comparison.

items.title: Visioning 2035: The future of the higher education sector in the UK
items.authors: Eddie Blass, Anne Jasman & Steve Shelley
items.why: A useful scenario-oriented paper on higher education futures. It gives background for comparing institutional futures over a long horizon.

items.title: The third mission of the university: A systematic literature review on potentials and constraints
items.why: Useful for university futures because it examines the third mission and the tensions around social, economic, and institutional roles of universities.

items.title: Only connect: Designing university futures
items.why: A recent and relevant piece for thinking about university futures with philosophical and institutional seriousness.

items.title: Augmentation or automation? The expanding role of artificial intelligence in urban planning and governance
items.authors: Frank Othengrafen, Lars Sievers, Merle Frankl
items.source: International Journal of Urban Sustainable Development. DOI: 10.1080/19463138.2026.2686016
items.why: Relevant as an empirical Delphi-based study of how AI is being positioned in urban planning and governance, especially the distinction between decision-support augmentation and automation.

items.title: Spatial implications of remote work: insights from seven European case studies
items.authors: Georgia Pozoukidou, Theodora Istoriou, Dimitra Plastara, Margarita Angelidou, Katharina Fellnhofer et al.
items.why: Useful for the futures-of-work strand because it treats remote work as a spatial and governance issue rather than only an individual employment preference.

items.title: Three theses of the future as a Science, Technology and Society Studies research object: current approaches and challenges
items.authors: Cristián Navarrete, Iván Ojeda-Pereira, Fernando Campos-Medina, Sebastián Herrera-León
items.source: Humanities and Social Sciences Communications. DOI: 10.1057/s41599-026-07541-0
items.why: A strong conceptual fit for the radar because it treats the future itself as an STS research object and connects futures studies with science and technology studies.
